name: Lijiang
summary: Lijiang is a remarkably safe destination for tourists, particularly compared to many international cities. The ancient town benefits from excellent security infrastructure, low crime rates, and a welcoming local population that is respectful toward international visitors.
quickVerdict: Lijiang stands out as one of China's safest tourist destinations, with violent crime being virtually nonexistent and petty theft rare even in crowded tourist areas. The city benefits from extensive CCTV coverage and strong police presence, making it particularly welcoming for solo travelers including women. While natural hazards like earthquakes and floods pose some regional risk, and tourists should remain aware of common scams, the overwhelming majority of visitors experience trouble-free stays in this UNESCO World Heritage city.

neighborhoods.name: Lijiang Old Town (UNESCO Core)
neighborhoods.description: The historic heart of Lijiang with excellent lighting, heavy foot traffic, and constant security presence. Tourist police patrol regularly and locals are accustomed to international visitors. Very safe day and night.

neighborhoods.name: Black Dragon Pool Area
neighborhoods.description: Popular scenic area with good infrastructure and regular tourist traffic. Well-maintained paths and adequate lighting make this area safe for solo exploration during daylight hours.

neighborhoods.name: Shuhe Ancient Town
neighborhoods.description: Quieter than the main old town but still well-maintained and safe. Some areas may be less crowded in the evening, so standard precautions are recommended after dark.

neighborhoods.name: New City Commercial District
neighborhoods.description: Modern area with hotels, restaurants and shopping. Generally safe but can be busier with traffic. Standard urban precautions apply, particularly around transportation hubs.

neighborhoods.name: Jade Dragon Snow Mountain Base
neighborhoods.description: Popular day trip destination with organized tour infrastructure. Main risks are altitude-related and weather conditions rather than crime. Follow guide instructions carefully.

neighborhoods.name: Rural Outskirts and Villages
neighborhoods.description: Remote areas with limited infrastructure and emergency services. While locals are generally friendly, language barriers and distance from help require extra caution for solo travelers.

scams.name: Tea House Ceremony Scam
scams.description: Friendly English-speaking 'students' approach tourists, particularly solo males, and invite them to traditional tea ceremonies that result in extremely inflated bills of hundreds of dollars.
scams.howToAvoid: Be wary of strangers with perfect English approaching in tourist areas. Never follow someone to a tea house or restaurant you didn't choose. Ask for menus with prices before ordering anything.

scams.name: Fake Art Student Scam
scams.description: Young people claiming to be art students befriend tourists and invite them to view and purchase artwork at galleries with high-pressure sales tactics and inflated prices.
scams.howToAvoid: Don't follow strangers to art galleries or exhibitions. Be suspicious of anyone approaching you with perfect English in tourist areas. Research galleries independently before visiting.

scams.name: Overpriced Tour Guide Scam
scams.description: Unlicensed guides offer cheap tours but take visitors only to shops where they earn commissions, or quote much higher prices than agreed upon after services are rendered.
scams.howToAvoid: Book tours through reputable agencies or hotels. Confirm all prices in writing before starting. Don't accept unsolicited tour offers from people approaching you on the street.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Some taxi drivers refuse to use meters or quote inflated flat rates, especially to obvious tourists or for trips to popular destinations.
scams.howToAvoid: Insist on meter usage or agree on fare before getting in. Use ride-hailing apps like Didi when available. Have your destination written in Chinese to avoid communication issues.

soloFemale.overview: Lijiang is exceptionally safe for solo female travelers, with harassment being rare and locals generally respectful and helpful toward international women. The city's extensive CCTV network and strong security presence provide additional peace of mind, while the tourism-focused economy means residents are accustomed to foreign visitors.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in the old town area for easy access to tourist police
Keep your phone charged and have hotel address in Chinese for taxi rides
Trust your instincts and avoid isolated areas after dark, though main tourist areas remain very safe

nightSafety.overview: Lijiang maintains good safety levels after dark, particularly in the main tourist areas which remain well-lit and populated. The old town has a vibrant nightlife scene with bars and restaurants operating late, creating natural foot traffic and informal security through numbers.
tips: Stick to well-lit main streets and tourist areas after dark
Avoid quiet, less-developed areas outside the main tourist zones
Keep valuables secure and maintain awareness in crowded nightlife areas

transport.metro: Lijiang does not have a metro system. The city relies on buses, taxis, and ride-hailing services for public transportation.
transport.rideshare: Didi (Chinese Uber) operates in Lijiang and is generally safe and reliable. Ensure you have the app set up with payment method before arrival. Drivers may not speak English.
transport.taxis: Official taxis are generally safe but insist on meter usage or agree on fare beforehand. Have destinations written in Chinese. Some drivers may try to overcharge tourists.
transport.tips: Transportation within the old town is mostly on foot. For longer distances, use official taxis or Didi. Airport transfers should be arranged through hotels when possible.

customs: Greet people with a nod or handshake rather than bowing
Present and receive items with both hands as a sign of respect
Remove shoes when entering traditional homes or certain indoor spaces
Avoid pointing with your finger - use an open hand gesture instead
Show extra respect to elderly people and wait for them to be served first

health.overview: Lijiang has adequate healthcare facilities for a tourist city, though more serious medical issues may require transfer to larger cities like Kunming. The high altitude (2400m) can cause problems for some visitors.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Stick to bottled water, which is widely available and inexpensive. Most hotels provide complimentary bottled water.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required beyond routine immunizations. Consider hepatitis A and B if planning extended stay or rural exploration.
health.altitude: Lijiang sits at 2400m elevation. Some visitors may experience mild altitude sickness. Ascend gradually, stay hydrated, and avoid alcohol on arrival. Seek medical attention if symptoms worsen.

emergency.touristPolice: Available in old town area - ask at major hotels or visitor centers
emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Beijing: +86-10-8531-4000 (24-hour emergency line). US Consulate Chengdu: +86-28-8558-3992 (covers Yunnan Province)

faq.a: Yes, Lijiang is very safe for tourists. It has low crime rates, extensive security coverage, and a tourism-focused economy where locals are accustomed to and welcoming toward international visitors.
The main tourist areas of Lijiang, particularly the old town, are safe at night with good lighting and regular foot traffic. Avoid isolated areas outside the main tourist zones after dark.

faq.q: Is Lijiang safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Lijiang is exceptionally safe for solo female travelers. Harassment is rare, locals are respectful, and the strong security presence provides additional peace of mind for women traveling alone.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Lijiang?
faq.a: While generally safe, avoid remote areas outside the tourist zones, particularly at night. Be cautious in rural villages where language barriers and distance from help could be problematic.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Lijiang?
faq.a: Public transport in Lijiang is generally safe. Use official taxis or the Didi app, ensure meters are used, and have destinations written in Chinese to avoid communication issues.
